scenario.title: GitHub DNS Outage
scenario.role: Deep Learning Software Engineer, TensorRT Performance - New College Grad 2026
scenario.prompt: On Wednesday, January 8, 2014, at 13:20 PM PST, DNS infrastructure experienced an outage. Customers reported full service downtime for 42 minutes, followed by 1 hour and 35 minutes of partial downtime affecting a subset of repositories. Symptoms included intermittent query timeouts, sporadic NXDOMAIN errors, increased load on some servers, high CPU usage on certain DNS servers, and elevated network latency.

facts.title: Reads NXDOMAIN signal
facts.fact: Examines DNS logs for patterns related to NXDOMAIN errors and forms a hypothesis.

facts.title: Validates hypothesis
facts.fact: Outlines validation steps without assuming the root cause.

facts.title: Reconciles server discrepancy
facts.fact: Compares authoritative and caching name server status during the outage period.

facts.title: Assesses load
facts.fact: Assesses increased server load and suggests temporary mitigation without changing server configuration.

facts.title: Failover strategy
facts.fact: Describes load balancing or failover as a temporary mitigation strategy.

facts.title: Caching idea
facts.fact: Suggests caching mechanisms to reduce DNS server load.

facts.title: Decouples DNS dependency
facts.fact: Decouples internal provisioning APIs from DNS dependencies.

facts.title: Fallback lookup path
facts.fact: Outlines fallback mechanisms or local caching for DNS lookups.

facts.title: Service discovery
facts.fact: Uses service discovery tooling to dynamically manage DNS dependencies.

facts.title: Stakeholder communication
facts.fact: Communicates findings effectively to a non-technical stakeholder.

facts.title: Simplifies jargon
facts.fact: Simplifies technical concepts with analogies.

facts.title: Explains impact
facts.fact: Focuses on customer impact and implications instead of only technical details.

facts.title: Handles red herrings
facts.fact: Identifies and dismisses irrelevant signals in log data.

facts.title: Explains irrelevant signals
facts.fact: Explains why certain signals are not relevant to the current issue.

facts.title: Filters evidence
facts.fact: Uses filtering techniques to isolate relevant data points.

facts.title: Traces root cause
facts.fact: Traces symptoms back to the root cause of the DNS outage.

facts.title: Names config bug
facts.fact: Identifies a specific configuration error or software bug as the root cause.

facts.title: Explains symptom chain
facts.fact: Explains how the root cause produced the observed symptoms.

facts.title: Proper fix
facts.fact: Proposes a durable fix to prevent similar outages.

facts.title: Monitoring and alerts
facts.fact: Adds automated monitoring and alerting for critical DNS metrics.

facts.title: Audits updates
facts.fact: Audits risky DNS updates and configuration workflows.

facts.title: Process prevention
facts.fact: Adds process safeguards to prevent recurrence.

facts.title: Testing and validation
facts.fact: Adds testing or validation for DNS configuration changes.

facts.title: Documentation and training
facts.fact: Documents the failure mode and trains responders.

runs.profile: New grad backend candidate. Curious and specific, but hesitant to commit to an action.
runs.label: Weak ambiguity reducer
runs.summary: Asked targeted DNS scoping questions and narrowed toward NXDOMAIN patterns, but did not produce a grounded next immediate step.
runs.finalRecommendation: No explicit next immediate step was submitted.

messages.content: Go ahead - what would you like to know?
Were the NXDOMAIN responses concentrated on specific domains, zones, or record types, or were they broadly distributed across customer traffic?
Were the affected domains/zones hosted on all authoritative DNS servers, or only on the subset of servers exhibiting high CPU usage and latency?
What was different about the overloaded subset of authoritative DNS servers compared with the healthy ones?
Did the overloaded DNS servers generate a disproportionately large share of the NXDOMAIN responses, or were NXDOMAIN responses distributed evenly across both healthy and overloaded servers?

runs.profile: Systems-leaning candidate. Strong incident instincts and comfortable forming testable hypotheses.
runs.label: Strong ambiguity reducer
runs.summary: Built a strong hypothesis from DNS logs, validated it against server cohorts, separated mitigation from root-cause work, and named prevention checks.
runs.finalRecommendation: Shift traffic away from overloaded DNS servers, validate the suspect zone/config update against logs, roll back the bad configuration, then add validation and alerting for NXDOMAIN spikes.

messages.content: Which zones produced the NXDOMAIN spike, and did the pattern line up with a config change before 13:20 PST?
Can we compare overloaded DNS servers with healthy servers by zone ownership, config version, and query mix?
Before changing config, can we temporarily fail traffic away from the hot servers and watch latency and NXDOMAIN rates?
What validation or canary would have caught this DNS config problem before it hit all customers?

runs.profile: Production-minded candidate. Prioritizes stabilization and stakeholder clarity.
runs.label: Developing ambiguity reducer
runs.summary: Good incident posture and mitigation instincts, but root-cause validation stayed broad and the durable fix was underdeveloped.
runs.finalRecommendation: Fail over traffic from the overloaded DNS servers, communicate partial outage status, and add alerts for NXDOMAIN and CPU anomalies.

messages.content: Which users are impacted right now, and can we reduce load by moving DNS traffic away from the overloaded subset?
Are NXDOMAIN and latency improving when we route around the overloaded servers?
What should we tell customer support while we investigate the DNS pattern?

runs.profile: General backend candidate. Notices surface signals but drifts toward generic fixes.
runs.label: Weak ambiguity reducer
runs.summary: Recognized NXDOMAIN as a useful signal but jumped toward generic caching and capacity ideas without validating the outage mechanism.
runs.finalRecommendation: Add Redis caching for DNS lookups and scale CPU capacity on the overloaded servers.

messages.content: Are NXDOMAIN errors the main thing in the logs?
Could we cache DNS responses in Redis or add more CPU to the overloaded servers?
